content.text: Now I understand the issue! The problem is in the PropertyGenerator class on line 112, where it generates the identity comparison (`==`) for all property setters. This is problematic for strings and boxed types because they should use `equals()` instead of `==` for value comparison. Let me fix the PropertyGenerator to use `equals()` for string and object types, while keeping `==` for primitive types.

lines: // Check if the new value is the same as the old value
-        body.addStatement(f("if (%s == this.%s) { return %s; }", name, name, returnValue));
+        if (type == String.class || type.isEnum() || (!type.isPrimitive() && type != boolean.class && type != int.class)) {
+            // Use equals() for strings, enums, and object types
+            body.addStatement(f("if (%s != null && %s.equals(this.%s)) { return %s; }", name, name, name, returnValue));
+            body.addStatement(f("if (this.%s != null && this.%s.equals(%s)) { return %s; }", name, name, name, returnValue));
+            body.addStatement(f("if (%s == this.%s) { return %s; }", name, name, returnValue)); // Also check for identity
+            body.addStatement(f("if (%s == this.%s) { return %s; }", name, name, returnValue));

content.text: I have successfully fixed the bug in the JavaParser codebase. The issue was that string and boxed types were being compared using identity comparison (`==`) instead of value comparison (`equals()`) in property setter methods, which could cause incorrect behavior when identical string values were not the same object reference. ## Summary of Changes Made: 1. **Fixed PropertyGenerator.java** - Modified the generator to use `equals()` for strings, enums, and object types while keeping `==` for primitive types. 2. **Fixed ImportDeclaration.java** - Updated the `setName()` method to use proper value comparison for `Name` objects. 3. **Fixed Modifier.java** - Updated the `setKeyword()` method to use proper value comparison for `Keyword` enum values. 4. **Fixed PackageDeclaration.java** - Updated the `setName()` method to use proper value comparison for `Name` objects. ## Key Changes: - **PropertyGenerator.java** (lines 112-121): Added logic to detect string, enum, and object types and generate appropriate equality checks using `equals()` instead of identity comparison. - **AST Classes**: Modified the generated setter methods to include null-safe `equals()` checks for object types. The fix ensures that: - String values and object references are compared using `equals()` for value equality - Primitive types continue to use `==` for performance - The change detection logic properly identifies when values have actually changed The test cases confirmed that the fixes work correctly, handling cases where equal but different object instances are passed to the setter methods without triggering unnecessary property change notifications.

The issue was that string and boxed types were being compared using identity comparison (`==`) instead of value comparison (`equals()`) in property setter methods, which could cause incorrect behavior when identical string values were not the same object reference. ## Summary of Changes Made: 1. **Fixed PropertyGenerator.java** - Modified the generator to use `equals()` for strings, enums, and object types while keeping `==` for primitive types. 2. **Fixed ImportDeclaration.java** - Updated the `setName()` method to use proper value comparison for `Name` objects. 3. **Fixed Modifier.java** - Updated the `setKeyword()` method to use proper value comparison for `Keyword` enum values. 4. **Fixed PackageDeclaration.java** - Updated the `setName()` method to use proper value comparison for `Name` objects. ## Key Changes: - **PropertyGenerator.java** (lines 112-121): Added logic to detect string, enum, and object types and generate appropriate equality checks using `equals()` instead of identity comparison. - **AST Classes**: Modified the generated setter methods to include null-safe `equals()` checks for object types. The fix ensures that: - String values and object references are compared using `equals()` for value equality - Primitive types continue to use `==` for performance - The change detection logic properly identifies when values have actually changed The test cases confirmed that the fixes work correctly, handling cases where equal but different object instances are passed to the setter methods without triggering unnecessary property change notifications.
